HMW-V05: FM Route Planning & Leg Management

How might we design the vendor's FM job detail page so the dispatcher can plan routes (create legs), assign drivers per leg, and monitor cargo progress through intermediate hubs, all in one cohesive experience?
The scenario: Admin creates an FM job: "Get TikTok cargo from Shenzhen to HK Airport. Assigned to HaleSun." HaleSun's dispatcher opens this job in the vendor app. They need to: (1) plan the route by adding intermediate hubs as legs, (2) assign a driver + vehicle to each leg, (3) later, monitor progress as drivers execute and hub ops scans at each stop. All of this lives on one page.

Design constraint: The page currently has Status Action Bar → Driver Assignment → Fee Breakdown → Route + Cargo → Proofs → Activity Log. We need to replace "Driver Assignment" and "Route" with a richer "Route Plan" section that handles legs. Fee reconciliation stays below (per HMW-V02 decision, fees-first is for reconciliation, but route planning comes first for FM because it's the primary action).

Option A: Vertical Timeline with Leg Cards

Recommended
Show the route as a vertical timeline. Each stop (origin, hub, hub, destination) is a node on the timeline. Between each pair of nodes is a "leg card" showing driver, vehicle, and status. Hub nodes show hub ops progress (ARR/PAL/DEP badges). Empty state has just origin + destination with an "Add Stop" button between them.

This reads top-to-bottom like the physical journey. The dispatcher scans the timeline and instantly sees: where the cargo is, which legs need drivers, which hubs are processing.
Pros
• Visually maps to the physical journey (top = start, bottom = end)
• Hub ops progress visible inline (ARR/PAL/DEP badges on hub nodes)
• Easy to add stops: "Add Stop" between any two nodes
• Driver assignment inline on each leg card
• Scales naturally: 2 legs or 6 legs, same pattern
Cons
• Tall: 4 hubs = 3 legs = ~400px of vertical space
• Different from the table-based design language elsewhere in the app
• Not a traditional "dense data" table pattern

State 1: New FM Job — No Legs Yet

← My Jobs J01 FM Trucking FM DO-2603-001 · TikTok · MAWB 160-84329871
Pending Plan your route to get started
Route Plan
TikTok WH, Shenzhen
Origin (from shipment)
+ Add intermediate stop to plan route
HK Airport
Final destination (from shipment)
No legs planned yet. Add intermediate stops (your hubs) between origin and destination to create legs. Each leg gets its own driver and vehicle assignment.
If no stops needed, this becomes a direct single-leg trip.

State 2: Route Planned — Assigning Drivers

← My Jobs J01 FM Trucking FM DO-2603-001 · TikTok · MAWB 160-84329871
Pending Assign drivers to each leg to dispatch
Route Plan · 3 Legs
TikTok WH, Shenzhen
30 Mar · 08:00
Leg 1
TikTok WH → Shenzhen Hub
Zhang Wei · 粤B·12345 5T
Planned
Shenzhen Hub (your hub)
Hub ops: inbound → processing → outbound
Leg 2 CROSS-BORDER
Shenzhen Hub → Huanggang Port
Li Ming · 粤B·67890 8T
Planned
Huanggang Port
EC: Customs clearance (handled by Gonda — separate job)
Leg 3
HK Hub → HK Airport
No driver assigned
Planned
HK Airport
30 Mar · 18:00 (estimated)

State 3: In Execution — Live Progress

← My Jobs J01 FM Trucking FM DO-2603-001 · TikTok · MAWB 160-84329871
In Progress Leg 1 delivered. Hub processing. Leg 2 waiting.
Route Plan · 3 Legs · Leg 1 Complete
TikTok WH, Shenzhen
30 Mar · 08:00
Leg 1 ✓
TikTok WH → Shenzhen Hub
Zhang Wei · 粤B·12345 · Delivered 30 Mar 10:42
Delivered
Shenzhen Hub
ARR ✓ 10:55 PAL In Progress DEP Waiting
Bags: 22/24 processed · 2 in queue
Leg 2 CROSS-BORDER
Shenzhen Hub → Huanggang Port
Li Ming · 粤B·67890
Waiting for hub
Huanggang Port
EC: Customs (Gonda)
Leg 3
HK Hub → HK Airport
Wang Jun · 港C·11111
Planned
HK Airport

Option B: Leg Table with Expandable Rows

Data Dense
Show legs as table rows. Each row: Leg # | Route | Driver | Vehicle | Status. Expanding a row shows hub ops progress for the destination hub. This follows the "tables not cards" design principle and is consistent with the rest of the vendor app.
Pros
• Consistent with the rest of the app (tables everywhere)
• More data dense — all legs visible in less vertical space
• Column-based scanning (driver column, status column)
• Familiar to spreadsheet users
Cons
• Loses the visual journey metaphor (top-to-bottom = start-to-end)
• Hub ops progress hidden in expandable rows (requires click to see)
• Harder to see the chain — which leg connects to which hub
• "Add Leg" interaction is less intuitive (insert row between rows?)
← My Jobs J01 FM Trucking FM DO-2603-001 · TikTok · MAWB 160-84329871
In Progress Leg 1 complete. Hub processing. 2 legs remaining.
Route Plan · 3 Legs
TikTok WH, Shenzhen → HK Airport 24 bags · 1,240 kg
# Route Driver Vehicle Hub Status Leg Status
1 TikTok WH → Shenzhen Hub Zhang Wei 粤B·12345 5T ARR ✓ PAL... Delivered
2 Shenzhen Hub → Huanggang Port Li Ming 粤B·67890 8T — Waiting
3 HK Hub → HK Airport Wang Jun 港C·11111 5T — Planned

Option C: Compact Timeline + Leg Table Below

Hybrid
Split the section in two. Top: a compact horizontal route bar showing the chain of stops (just dots + names, no leg details). Below: a leg table with all the details (driver, vehicle, status, hub ops). The route bar gives the visual journey, the table gives the data.
Pros
• Route bar gives instant visual overview of the chain
• Table below gives data density for each leg
• Best of both: journey metaphor + spreadsheet scanning
• Route bar is compact (one line), table handles the details
Cons
• Two representations of the same data (route bar + table) — possible confusion
• Horizontal route bar breaks at 5+ stops on narrow screens
• More complex to build (two coordinated components)
← My Jobs J01 FM Trucking FM DO-2603-001 · TikTok · MAWB 160-84329871
In Progress Leg 1 delivered. Hub processing.
Route Plan · 3 Legs
TikTok WH
✓ Picked up
SZ Hub
Processing
Huanggang
Customs
HK Hub
HK Airport
# Route Driver Vehicle Hub Status
1 TikTok WH → SZ Hub Zhang Wei 粤B·12345 PAL... Delivered
2 SZ Hub → Huanggang Li Ming 粤B·67890 — Waiting
3 HK Hub → HK Airport Wang Jun 港C·11111 — Planned

Recommendation: Option A — Vertical Timeline

Option A wins because route planning is fundamentally a journey, not a spreadsheet. The dispatcher thinks "cargo goes from here, to here, to here." A vertical timeline maps directly to that mental model. Top = start, bottom = end. Each stop is a node. Each movement between stops is a leg card.

The killer feature is hub ops visibility inline. When the dispatcher sees "Shenzhen Hub: ARR ✓ · PAL In Progress · DEP Waiting," they instantly know the cargo is being processed and leg 2 can't start yet. In Option B (table), this is hidden in an expandable row. In Option C (hybrid), it's split across two representations.

Yes, it's taller than a table. But the dispatcher is on this page to MANAGE a multi-leg journey, not scan 50 rows. They're looking at one FM job with 3-5 legs. The vertical space is justified by the information density per node.

Option B is the "safe, consistent" choice but loses the journey metaphor. Option C is clever but shows the same data twice. Option A is the one that makes the dispatcher say "I can see exactly where my cargo is."
